summary: French Cubist painter and theorist, best known for co-writing Du "Cubisme" (On Cubism) with Jean Metzinger — the first major theoretical treatise on Cubism. Associated with Crystal Cubism and the Section d'Or (Puteaux Group). Key paintings include The Bathers (reproduced in Du Cubisme), Portrait of Jacques Nayral, La Chasse (The Hunt), Woman with Phlox, and Harvest Threshing.

works.name: Du "Cubisme" (On Cubism, 1912)
works.description: The first major theoretical manifesto on Cubism, co-written with Jean Metzinger. Argues that Cubism is grounded in non-Euclidean/Riemannian geometry. Reproduces Gleizes's painting The Bathers. Wikipedia

clues.clue: First/major theoretical treatise on Cubism
examples: Jean Metzinger collaborated with this artist and philosopher on the first theoretical treatise on Cubism.

clues.clue: Argues Cubism is based on non-Euclidean/Riemannian geometry; correlates more with Riemannian than Euclidean geometry
examples: This work argues that its subject correlates more with non-Euclidean geometry than Euclidean geometry; thus, it would be helpful to understand some Riemannian theorems.
Along with Albert Gleizes, Metzinger argued that this movement's art was based in Riemannian geometry in their manifesto.

examples: Its authors, Albert Gleizes and Jean Metzinger, began collaborating after the Salon d'Automne.

clues.clue: Apollinaire's The Cubist Painters described Gleizes as representing the 'scientific' branch; Delaunay's as 'orphic'
examples: In one of his works, this critic describes artists such as Albert Gleizes and Robert Delaunay as practitioners of 'a pure art,' which he termed Orphism.

This critic discusses works which borrow 'not from the reality of sight, but from the reality of insight' in his essay 'On Painting,' which examines the 'scientific' work of Albert Gleizes and the 'orphic' style of Robert Delaunay.

works.name: The Bathers (Les Baigneurs, 1912)
works.description: A large Cubist painting depicting bathers in front of factories with swirling smoke in Clichy in the background. One of five Gleizes paintings depicting La Grande Jatte. Exhibited at the 1912 Salon des Independants. Reproduced in Du Cubisme. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Depicts bathers in front of factories producing swirling smoke (Clichy factories in background)
examples: Smoke from industrial factories in Clichy can be seen in the background of one painting of this location.
An Albert Gleizes painting titled for this activity, reproduced in Du 'Cubisme,' depicts it in front of factories producing swirling smoke.

clues.clue: Exhibited at the 1912 Salon des Independants
examples: Gleizes' painting The Bathers was exhibited at the 1912 edition of this salon, founded in 1884 in opposition to the government-sponsored Salon.

clues.clue: One of five Gleizes depictions of La Grande Jatte / set at La Grande Jatte
examples: The Bathers was among five depictions of La Grand Jatte by this artist.

clues.clue: Reproduced in Du Cubisme
examples: This artist reproduced The Bathers for his manifesto Du Cubisme.

works.name: Harvest Threshing (1912)
works.description: One of the largest Cubist paintings, equaled only by Robert Delaunay's La Ville de Paris. Wikipedia

works.name: Section d'Or (Puteaux Group)
works.description: A Cubist collective associated with Cubism and Orphism, of which Gleizes was a founding member. Section d'Or meetings were held at Gleizes's home. The name 'Section d'Or' (Golden Section) was coined by Josephin Peladan due to fascination with phi. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Associated with Cubism and Orphism; Gleizes and Metzinger were members
examples: Lacking Braque and Picasso's exclusive deal with Daniel Kahnweiler, this Cubist group exhibited their work in salons. Albert Gleizes and Jean Metzinger were part of this group with a colorful, geometrically inspired name.
Metzinger was a co-founder of this artist collective associated with Cubism and Orphism.

comprehensive_summary: Albert Gleizes was a French Cubist painter and theorist, best known for co-writing Du "Cubisme" (On Cubism, 1912) with Jean Metzinger — the first major theoretical manifesto on Cubism. The text argues that Cubist art is grounded in non-Euclidean (Riemannian) geometry rather than Euclidean geometry, and Gleizes and Metzinger began collaborating after the Salon d'Automne. Guillaume Apollinaire, in his 1913 book The Cubist Painters: Aesthetic Meditations, described Gleizes as representing the 'scientific' branch of Cubism, in contrast to Robert Delaunay's 'orphic' style. The manifesto reproduced Gleizes's large painting The Bathers, which depicts working-class bathers in front of factories with swirling smoke from Clichy in the background; it was set at La Grande Jatte (one of five Gleizes paintings of that location) and was exhibited at the 1912 Salon des Independants. Other key works include Portrait of Jacques Nayral (a frequent giveaway identifier), La Chasse (The Hunt) — depicting a man with a golden hunting horn and hunting dogs — Woman with Phlox (described as almost entirely grey), and Harvest Threshing, one of the largest Cubist paintings alongside Delaunay's La Ville de Paris. Gleizes was an advocate of Crystal Cubism. He was a founding member of the Section d'Or (Golden Section, also known as the Puteaux Group), a Cubist collective associated with both Cubism and Orphism whose meetings were held at Gleizes's home; the name was coined by Josephin Peladan for his fascination with the quantity phi. Gleizes and Metzinger infamously rejected Marcel Duchamp's Nude Descending a Staircase No. 2 from the 1912 Salon des Independants, believing the nude should be shown reclining rather than in motion. Gleizes also chronicled the fortunes of the Salon d'Automne in a work called The Epic: From Immobile Form to Mobile Form. He and Jacques Villon made money from the 1913 Armory Show. The forger John Myatt began his career after his mountain village painting in Gleizes's style was accepted by Christie's. In one painting, Gleizes depicted a woman whose hair is rendered as concentric black-and-white arcs, and who holds a black glove.
recursive_suggestions: Jean Metzinger — co-author of Du Cubisme; closely associated in nearly all Gleizes clues
Section d'Or / Puteaux Group — appears 3+ times; Gleizes was a founding member
Du Cubisme (as a work) — if you want a standalone analysis of the manifesto itself
Guillaume Apollinaire — described Gleizes in The Cubist Painters; appears in text mentions
